title: Predecessors

lean_statement.name: predLt
predLe

lean_explanation:
The encoding takes a CDMG `G : CDMG α` (a Conditional Directed Mixed Graph over an ambient vertex type `α : Type*`), a binary relation `r : α → α → Prop` playing the role of the LN's strict total order $<$, and a target node `v : α`. The relation is *renamed* from $<$ because Lean reserves `<` for the typeclass-resolved order; and the relation is deliberately *not* required to be a topological order at the definitional layer — see the design notes for why.

Two parallel definitions are introduced, both returning a `Set α` whose body is set-builder notation. `predLt G r v` is the LN's strict form $\Pred^G_<(v) = \{w \in G \mid w < v\}$: the set of `w : α` satisfying `w ∈ G ∧ r w v`. Here `w ∈ G` uses the CDMG-specific `Membership` instance set up in `CDMGNotation.lean`, which unfolds (by `CDMG.mem_iff`) to `w ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` — that is, `w` is either an input or a vertex of `G`. `predLe G r v` is the LN's weak form $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \{w \in G \mid w < v\} \cup \{v\}$: the same set-builder set, unioned with the singleton `{v}`. The union with `{v}` is unconditional — the LN literally writes $\cup \{v\}$ with no `v ∈ G` guard, so `v` lands in `predLe G r v` even when it is not a node of `G`.

Two companion `@[simp]` lemmas `mem_predLt` and `mem_predLe` unfold each definition for downstream rewriting: `w ∈ G.predLt r v ↔ w ∈ G ∧ r w v` (by `Iff.rfl`, since set-builder notation reduces definitionally) and `w ∈ G.predLe r v ↔ (w ∈ G ∧ r w v) ∨ w = v` (by `Set.mem_union` together with `Set.mem_singleton_iff`).
design_choices:
**Relation `r : α → α → Prop`, not a `LinearOrder` on a subtype.** The LN writes $w < v$ for bare vertices $w, v$, so the relation should accept the same vertex type that everything else in the formalisation (`Pa G w`, `v ∈ G`, walks, …) reads. The natural-looking alternative — a `LinearOrder` on the subtype $\,↥(G.J \cup G.V)$ — would force every downstream $w < v$ statement to first inject `w` and `v` into the subtype, breaking the LN's bare-vertex notation (cf. the id-algorithm chapter's $v_1 < v_2 < v_3$ prose). Keeping `r` as a binary relation on the full carrier `α` also lets the same `r` be reused against substituted graphs $G_{\doit(C^\cmpl)}$ — predecessors there are just `predLt G' r v` for the new graph `G'` — without having to thread a new order instance through the substitution.

**`Set α`, not `Finset α`.** Every downstream use of predecessors treats them as a *set*: intersection with ambient subsets in the id-algorithm chapter, big unions across an index in scms4, indexing families of co-domains in scms3, and indexing families of random variables in the Markov-property proof. Forcing `Finset` would either impose `DecidableEq α` everywhere (later chapters use real-valued vertices, for which decidable equality is unavailable) or require a global `Fintype (G.J ∪ G.V)` hypothesis the LN does not impose.

**No `IsTopologicalOrder G r` precondition baked into the signature.** The LN's "let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$" is documentational: the set $\{w \in G \mid w < v\}$ is well-defined for *any* binary relation. Bolting an order hypothesis into the signature would force every caller to thread a proof witness for the same `Set`, and would block using `predLt` inside the *statement* of claim_3_2 (where the existence of a topological order is the conclusion, not a hypothesis). Callers that genuinely need the order request it separately, exactly as the LN does.

**Body of `predLe` is `{w | w ∈ G ∧ r w v} ∪ {v}`, not `{w | w ∈ G ∧ (r w v ∨ w = v)}`.** The two forms differ exactly when `v ∉ G`: the former still adds `v`, the latter silently drops it. The LN literally writes $\cup \{v\}$ with no `v ∈ G` guard, so we follow the LN. Downstream callers that have `v ∈ G` in context (the common case in the id-algorithm chapter) get the equivalent set via `mem_predLe`; callers that don't get exactly what the LN gives them. Re-using `predLt` itself (`predLt G r v ∪ {v}`) would be propositionally equivalent; inlining the set-builder keeps the definitional body character-for-character aligned with the LN equation.

**`v ∈ G` (the `Membership` instance from `CDMGNotation.lean`), not `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`.** The LN literally writes $w \in G$. The membership instance unfolds to $w \in G.J \cup G.V$ propositionally (`CDMG.mem_iff`), so the two are interchangeable in proofs — but writing `v ∈ G` keeps the Lean source literally aligned with the LN's prose.

**Names `predLt` / `predLe`, not `Pred` or `Pred_lt`.** `Pred` alone clashes with core Lean / Mathlib (`Nat.pred`, `Order.Pred`, …) and would generate disambiguation noise at every use site. The camelCase `predLt` / `predLe` mirrors Mathlib's conventions (`Set.image`, `Set.preimage`, `Finset.range`) and stays recognisable as the LN's $\Pred^G_<$ / $\Pred^G_\le$.
